name: Acute Subdural Hematoma
clinical: Altered mental status or focal neurologic deficit following head trauma, often with brief lucid interval.
imaging: Crescent-shaped hyperdensity conforming to brain surface on CT; may cross suture lines.
report: Acute subdural hematoma with mass effect and midline shift concerning for herniation.
treatment: Neurosurgery consultation for possible emergent evacuation; manage increased ICP.

name: Epidural Hematoma
clinical: Classic 'talk and die' presentation with brief loss of consciousness, lucidity, then rapid deterioration.
imaging: Lens-shaped hyperdensity typically crossing midline; does not cross suture lines.
report: Acute epidural hematoma with significant mass effect and herniation risk.
treatment: Emergent neurosurgery consultation for craniotomy and hematoma evacuation.

name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
clinical: Sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache, neck stiffness, photophobia; may have aneurysmal or traumatic etiology.
imaging: Hyperdensity within sulci and basal cisterns on non-contrast CT; may show aneurysm on CTA.
report: Acute subarachnoid hemorrhage with hyperdensity in basal cisterns; CTA recommended to identify source.
treatment: ICU admission, blood pressure control, nimodipine for vasospasm prevention; neurosurgery/interventional radiology consult.

name: Acute Ischemic Stroke
clinical: Acute neurologic deficit (facial droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty) within critical thrombolytic window.
imaging: CT may be normal early; MRI DWI shows acute ischemic territory; CTA/MRA reveals arterial occlusion.
report: Acute ischemia in distribution of middle cerebral artery with proximal arterial occlusion.
treatment: Neurology consultation for thrombolysis eligibility; consider thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion.

name: Hemorrhagic Stroke
clinical: Acute focal neurologic deficit with headache and hypertension; higher risk of herniation than ischemic stroke.
imaging: Hyperdensity within brain parenchyma on CT; may extend into ventricles or subarachnoid space.
report: Acute intracerebral hemorrhage in basal ganglia/thalamic region with significant mass effect.
treatment: ICU admission, aggressive BP control, coagulopathy reversal if indicated; neurosurgery consult for mass effect.

name: Acute Epidural Abscess/Meningitis
clinical: Fever, meningeal signs, altered mental status; may have recent spinal procedure or infection.
imaging: MRI shows enhancement of dura or epidural space; restricted diffusion if abscess present.
report: Epidural enhancement with restricted diffusion consistent with abscess versus inflammatory changes.
treatment: Infectious disease and neurosurgery consultation; IV antibiotics; possible emergency drainage.

name: Tension Pneumocephalus
clinical: Altered mental status and headache following head trauma or neurosurgery; progressive neurologic deterioration.
imaging: Large air collection with mass effect and ventricular compression on CT.
report: Tension pneumocephalus with significant mass effect and midline shift indicating elevated pressure.
treatment: Neurosurgery consultation; may require needle aspiration or burr hole drainage.

name: Brainstem Stroke
clinical: Altered consciousness, cranial nerve palsies, bilateral motor/sensory findings; high mortality if basilar artery.
imaging: MRI DWI shows restricted diffusion in brainstem; CTA may reveal basilar artery occlusion.
report: Acute ischemia in brainstem distribution consistent with basilar artery territory infarction.
treatment: Emergent thrombolysis or thrombectomy consideration; ICU-level supportive care.

name: Cerebellar Hemorrhage with Infarction
clinical: Ataxia, headache, vomiting, altered consciousness; rapid deterioration from brainstem compression.
imaging: Hemorrhage or infarction in cerebellar region with fourth ventricle compression and obstructive hydrocephalus.
report: Acute cerebellar hemorrhage with mass effect on fourth ventricle and early hydrocephalus.
treatment: Neurosurgery consultation for possible decompression; external ventricular drain consideration.

name: Acute Hydrocephalus
clinical: Altered mental status, headache, upgaze paresis, ataxia; occurs with ventricular outflow obstruction.
imaging: Ventricular enlargement disproportionate to sulcal effacement; transependymal CSF signal on MRI.
report: Acute obstructive hydrocephalus with enlarged ventricles and transependymal CSF leak.
treatment: Neurosurgery consultation for emergent ventriculostomy or external ventricular drain placement.

name: Herniation Syndrome
clinical: Deteriorating consciousness with ipsilateral pupil dilation and contralateral motor weakness; imminent death without intervention.
imaging: Midline shift with compression of lateral ventricles and transfalcine or transtentorial herniation on imaging.
report: Significant mass effect with evidence of transtentorial herniation and brainstem compression.
treatment: Emergent neurosurgery; ICU support; head-of-bed elevation and osmotic therapy temporizing measures.

name: Acute Tension Pneumothorax
clinical: Acute dyspnea, hypotension, JVD, tracheal deviation; life-threatening respiratory emergency.
imaging: Complete lung collapse with mediastinal shift, absent lung markings on affected side.
report: Tension pneumothorax with complete ipsilateral lung collapse and severe mediastinal shift.
treatment: Emergent needle decompression followed by chest tube placement; no delay for imaging.

name: Hemothorax
clinical: Dyspnea and hypotension after trauma; may rapidly become hemodynamically unstable.
imaging: Layering opacity in dependent lung with possible mediastinal shift; echogenic fluid on ultrasound.
report: Large hemothorax with mass effect and significant hemodynamic impact.
treatment: Chest tube placement; ICU monitoring for ongoing hemorrhage; surgical consultation if massive bleeding.

name: Flail Chest with Pulmonary Contusion
clinical: Multiple rib fractures with paradoxical movement; high risk for respiratory failure from underlying lung injury.
imaging: Multiple rib fractures on radiograph; ground-glass opacities indicating pulmonary contusion on CT.
report: Flail chest with extensive pulmonary contusion and alveolar consolidation.
treatment: Aggressive pain control, incentive spirometry, monitor for respiratory decompensation; ICU if severe.

name: Tension Pneumomediastinum
clinical: Chest pain, dyspnea, hypotension after trauma or with barotrauma; may result in cardiac tamponade.
imaging: Air in mediastinal tissues with compression of great vessels and cardiac chambers on CT.
report: Tension pneumomediastinum with significant vascular compression and hemodynamic compromise.
treatment: Emergent needle or catheter decompression of mediastinum; tube thoracostomy if pneumothorax present.

name: Acute Aortic Dissection
clinical: Sudden severe chest or back pain radiating to interscapular region with hypertension; high mortality if untreated.
imaging: Intimal flap in aorta with true and false lumens on CTA; confirms site and extent of dissection.
report: Type A aortic dissection extending to ascending aorta with pericardial effusion concerning for rupture.
treatment: Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consultation; immediate BP and heart rate control to reduce aortic stress.

name: Rupturing/Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
clinical: Hypotension, pulsatile abdominal mass, flank pain; mortality exceeds 50% even with surgery.
imaging: AAA >5.5 cm with contained rupture or free rupture with retroperitoneal/intraperitoneal blood.
report: Large AAA with contained rupture and sentinel bleeding into retroperitoneal space.
treatment: Emergent vascular surgery consult; type and cross; permissive hypotension; operating room immediately.

name: Acute Pulmonary Embolism
clinical: Acute dyspnea and chest pain with acute cor pulmonale; risk of hemodynamic collapse and death.
imaging: CTA shows filling defect in pulmonary arteries; may see right heart strain and elevated troponin.
report: Massive bilateral pulmonary embolism with acute right heart strain and elevated RV/LV ratio.
treatment: Anticoagulation; consider thrombolysis for massive PE; interventional radiology for embolectomy if indicated.

name: Acute Myocardial Infarction with Rupture
clinical: Sudden hypotension and shock in setting of acute MI; mechanical complication with high mortality.
imaging: Free pericardial fluid with hemopericardium on CT or echocardiography.
report: Large pericardial effusion consistent with ventricular rupture in setting of acute myocardial infarction.
treatment: Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consult; supportive measures; operating room for repair.

name: Tension Cardiac Tamponade
clinical: Hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds; life-threatening compression of cardiac function.
imaging: Large pericardial effusion with diastolic collapse of right atrium on ultrasound.
report: Massive pericardial effusion with right atrial and ventricular diastolic collapse indicating tamponade physiology.
treatment: Emergent pericardiocentesis for relief; search for underlying etiology; ICU monitoring.

name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
clinical: Severe abdominal pain disproportionate to exam findings; high mortality if diagnosis delayed.
imaging: CT shows bowel wall thinning, lack of enhancement, and mesenteric arterial occlusion or severe stenosis.
report: Acute superior mesenteric artery occlusion with non-enhancing small bowel and pneumatosis intestinalis.
treatment: Emergent vascular surgery/interventional radiology consult; consider angiography and revascularization.

name: Boerhaave Syndrome (Esophageal Rupture)
clinical: Chest or abdominal pain after forceful vomiting; rapid progression to sepsis and death without treatment.
imaging: Pneumomediastinum, mediastinal widening, and pleural effusion on radiograph; CT shows esophageal defect.
report: Pneumomediastinum with mediastinal gas and cervical subcutaneous emphysema consistent with esophageal rupture.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; avoid oral intake; broad-spectrum antibiotics; operative repair needed.

name: Acute Pancreatitis with Necrosis
clinical: Severe epigastric pain radiating to back with elevated amylase; risk of multiorgan failure and death.
imaging: Pancreatic enlargement with heterogeneous enhancement, fluid collections, and peripancreatic stranding.
report: Severe acute pancreatitis with extensive pancreatic necrosis and retroperitoneal fluid collections.
treatment: ICU admission, aggressive hydration, NPO status; monitor for infected necrosis requiring drainage.

name: Perforated Peptic Ulcer
clinical: Acute severe epigastric pain with free air under diaphragm; requires emergent surgery.
imaging: Pneumoperitoneum on upright chest radiograph or CT; may visualize ulcer crater.
report: Pneumoperitoneum indicating hollow viscus perforation; likely perforated gastric ulcer.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; NPO status; IV fluids; antibiotics; operating room for repair.

name: Bowel Obstruction (Strangulated)
clinical: Abdominal pain and distension with vomiting; strangulation risk with rapid progression to perforation.
imaging: Dilated bowel loops with transition point; CT shows closed-loop obstruction and ischemic changes.
report: Strangulated small bowel obstruction with evidence of ischemia and pneumatosis.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; NG tube; IV fluids; likely requires operative intervention.

name: Appendicitis with Perforation
clinical: Right lower quadrant pain with fever and peritoneal signs; perforation leads to peritonitis.
imaging: Non-compressible appendix >6 mm with periappendiceal fluid and free intraperitoneal air if perforated.
report: Perforated appendicitis with appendicolith and loculated periappendiceal abscess.
treatment: Surgical consultation; emergent appendectomy or percutaneous drainage if contained perforation.

name: Acute Diverticulitis with Perforation
clinical: Left lower quadrant pain and fever; perforation results in fecal peritonitis.
imaging: Diverticula with adjacent inflammation, fluid collection, and free air if perforated.
report: Perforated acute diverticulitis with large abscess and free peritoneal air.
treatment: Surgical consultation; NPO; antibiotics; possible emergency colostomy or primary repair.

name: Toxic Megacolon
clinical: Severe colitis with toxic appearance and colonic dilation; risk of perforation and death.
imaging: Colon >6 cm diameter on radiograph; CT shows severe colonic inflammation and markedly dilated segments.
report: Toxic megacolon with colon diameter >8 cm and severe mucosal inflammation.
treatment: Gastroenterology and surgery consultation; aggressive medical management; surgery if perforation or no improvement.

name: Acute Mesenteric Venous Thrombosis
clinical: Abdominal pain with vomiting; insidious presentation with high mortality if missed.
imaging: Thrombus in mesenteric veins with intestinal wall thickening and ascites.
report: Mesenteric venous thrombosis with early ischemic changes in dependent bowel.
treatment: Anticoagulation; monitor for progression; consider intervention if bowel viability questionable.

name: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy
clinical: Hypotension, severe pelvic pain, positive pregnancy test; can be rapidly fatal without surgical intervention.
imaging: Free pelvic/peritoneal blood with corpus luteum cyst and no intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound.
report: Large amount of free pelvic fluid consistent with ruptured ectopic pregnancy.
treatment: Emergent OB/GYN consultation; type and cross; operating room for exploratory surgery and hemostasis.

name: Ovarian Torsion
clinical: Acute pelvic pain and vomiting; risk of ovarian loss if not rapidly detorted.
imaging: Enlarged ovary with peripheral follicles and echogenic stroma on ultrasound; venous flow absent.
report: Markedly enlarged right ovary with peripheral cystic follicles and no venous Doppler flow indicating torsion.
treatment: Emergent OB/GYN consultation; operative detorsion or oophorectomy if necrotic.

name: Ruptured Hemorrhagic Ovarian Cyst
clinical: Acute pelvic pain and hemodynamic compromise; mimics ectopic pregnancy or surgical abdomen.
imaging: Large cystic structure with internal hemorrhage and free fluid in pelvis.
report: Ruptured hemorrhagic ovarian cyst with significant free intraperitoneal blood.
treatment: Pelvic rest, analgesia, serial imaging; surgery if hemodynamic instability or continued bleeding.

name: Acute Pyelonephritis with Urosepsis
clinical: Flank pain, fever, and shock; sepsis can rapidly progress to multiorgan failure.
imaging: Wedge-shaped area of diminished enhancement in kidney; CT shows collecting system dilatation.
report: Acute pyelonephritis with septal stranding and hydronephrosis from obstructing stone.
treatment: Urologic consultation; antibiotics; percutaneous nephrostomy if obstructed and septic.

name: Perinephric Abscess
clinical: Fever, flank pain, and sepsis; can result from complicated pyelonephritis or hematogenous spread.
imaging: Fluid collection surrounding kidney with rim enhancement on CT.
report: Large perinephric abscess with fluid locules and thick enhancing rim.
treatment: Percutaneous drainage and antibiotics; address underlying urologic obstruction if present.

name: Spontaneous Renal Artery Dissection
clinical: Flank pain and hematuria with elevated creatinine; can cause acute renal infarction.
imaging: Intimal flap in renal artery with wedge-shaped area of non-enhancement in kidney.
report: Renal artery dissection with acute infarction of dependent renal parenchyma.
treatment: Anticoagulation consideration; monitor renal function; interventional radiology for endovascular repair if compromised.

name: Acute Renal Infarction
clinical: Flank pain and hematuria; often missed because presentation mimics renal colic.
imaging: Wedge-shaped area of non-enhancement in renal artery distribution; elevated LDH.
report: Acute renal infarction in left upper pole kidney with minimal renal artery involvement.
treatment: Anticoagulation; address underlying cause (dissection, thromboembolism, arrhythmia).

name: Fournier Gangrene
clinical: Rapidly progressive genital pain and systemic toxicity; high mortality without emergent surgical intervention.
imaging: Gas in soft tissues of perineum and genital region with extensive inflammation on CT.
report: Extensive subcutaneous emphysema in perineal and genital tissues consistent with necrotizing infection.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation for debridement; broad-spectrum antibiotics; likely multiple operations needed.

name: Testicular Torsion
clinical: Acute testicular pain; testis viable only if treated within 6 hours; must rule out before ultrasound.
imaging: Absent or reversed flow on color Doppler ultrasound; testis may appear heterogeneous.
report: Right testis with absent venous Doppler flow and heterogeneous echotexture consistent with torsion.
treatment: Emergent urology consultation for manual detorsion; operative bilateral fixation required.

name: Ischemic Colitis
clinical: Bloody diarrhea and abdominal pain in elderly; can progress rapidly to transmural necrosis.
imaging: Segmental colonic wall thickening and pericolonic fat stranding; may show thumbprinting.
report: Acute ischemic colitis with watershed zone involvement and early transmural changes.
treatment: NPO, IV fluids, antibiotics; serial abdominal exams; surgery if signs of perforation or necrosis.

name: Internal Hernia (Post-Bariatric)
clinical: Intermittent or acute bowel obstruction in post-bariatric patient; high risk of strangulation.
imaging: Clustered small bowel loops in abnormal location with mesenteric vessel torsion.
report: Internal hernia with strangulated small bowel loops and compromised vascular perfusion.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; operative reduction or repair of hernia defect.

name: Abdominal Wall Hematoma (Rectus Sheath)
clinical: Pulsatile mass and pain on exam; can rapidly expand and cause hypovolemic shock.
imaging: Heterogeneous fluid collection within rectus muscle on ultrasound; CT shows arterial bleeding source.
report: Large expanding hematoma within right rectus abdominis with active arterial bleeding.
treatment: Vascular/surgical consultation; observe if stable; percutaneous drainage or embolization if bleeding.

name: Acute Arterial Mesenteric Occlusion (Embolic)
clinical: Sudden severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam in patient with atrial fibrillation.
imaging: Abrupt cutoff of superior mesenteric artery with non-enhancing bowel segments.
report: Superior mesenteric artery embolism with acute non-enhancing ischemic bowel.
treatment: Emergent vascular surgery/interventional radiology; catheter thrombectomy or surgical embolectomy.

name: Acute Arterial Mesenteric Occlusion (Thrombotic)
clinical: Gradual abdominal pain in patient with atherosclerotic disease; prodromal symptoms common.
imaging: Severe stenosis or occlusion of mesenteric vessels with proximal collateral development.
report: Chronic mesenteric ischemia with acute thrombotic occlusion and non-perfusing bowel.
treatment: Vascular surgery consultation; emergent revascularization or bypass; monitor for viability.

name: Superior Vena Cava Syndrome
clinical: Facial swelling, upper extremity edema, venous distension; acute presentation suggests thrombosis or PE.
imaging: SVC occlusion with collateral venous development and mediastinal mass on CT.
report: Complete SVC obstruction from mediastinal malignancy with extensive collateral circulation.
treatment: Oncology/vascular consultation; stent placement or chemotherapy depending on etiology.

name: Acute Adrenal Crisis
clinical: Hypotension, altered mental status, and shock in steroid-dependent patient or with adrenal pathology.
imaging: Enlarged bilateral adrenal glands with acute hemorrhage or infarction on CT.
report: Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage consistent with acute adrenal crisis.
treatment: High-dose IV glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids immediately; ICU support; address underlying cause.

name: Nephrotic Syndrome with Renal Vein Thrombosis
clinical: Massive proteinuria and acute kidney injury; thrombosis can rapidly lead to renal loss.
imaging: Thrombus in renal vein with lack of enhancement on Doppler ultrasound or CT.
report: Acute renal vein thrombosis with enlarged swollen kidney and diminished perfusion.
treatment: Anticoagulation; thrombolysis consideration; address underlying nephrotic syndrome.

name: Cauda Equina Syndrome
clinical: Bilateral leg pain, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction; neurosurgical emergency.
imaging: Large central disc herniation compressing cauda equina on lumbar MRI.
report: Large central lumbar disc herniation with severe compression of cauda equina nerve roots.
treatment: Emergent neurosurgery consultation; MRI confirmation; urgent decompressive laminectomy.

name: Spinal Epidural Hematoma
clinical: Acute back pain and rapidly progressive neurologic deficit; can cause permanent paralysis if not decompressed.
imaging: Hyperdense collection in epidural space compressing spinal cord on MRI or CT.
report: Large spinal epidural hematoma from T3-T6 with significant spinal cord compression.
treatment: Emergent neurosurgery consultation; emergent MRI; urgent laminectomy and hematoma evacuation.

name: Spinal Cord Compression from Metastasis
clinical: Back pain progressing to neurologic deficit in cancer patient; delays in treatment lead to permanent paralysis.
imaging: Vertebral body metastasis with epidural extension compressing spinal cord on MRI.
report: Metastatic disease at L2 with significant epidural involvement and spinal cord compression.
treatment: Emergent oncology/neurosurgery/radiation oncology consultation; consider emergent decompression or XRT.

name: Acute Myelitis
clinical: Acute spinal pain and progressive paralysis; can be viral, post-infectious, or MS-related.
imaging: T2 hyperintensity within spinal cord over multiple levels with T1 enhancement.
report: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis with extensive spinal cord signal abnormality.
treatment: High-dose IV methylprednisolone; neuro/infectious disease consultation; search for etiology.

name: Anterior Spinal Artery Infarction
clinical: Acute motor deficit and loss of pain/temperature sensation below level of lesion; prognosis poor.
imaging: T2 hyperintensity in ventral spinal cord in ASAD distribution.
report: Acute infarction in anterior spinal artery territory with ventral cord signal change.
treatment: Supportive care; investigate vascular cause; neuro consultation.

name: Vertebral Artery Dissection
clinical: Neck pain and subsequent stroke; can present with lateral medullary syndrome.
imaging: Intimal flap in vertebral artery on CTA or MRA with possible downstream ischemia.
report: Vertebral artery dissection with intimal flap and posterolateral medullary ischemia.
treatment: Anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy; neurology consultation; monitor for thromboembolic events.

name: Pneumatosis Intestinalis with Portal Venous Gas
clinical: Sign of bowel necrosis and perforation; indicates critical ischemic disease with high mortality.
imaging: Gas within bowel wall and portal vein branches on CT.
report: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas indicating transmural bowel necrosis.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; operative exploration for extent of necrosis and resection.

name: Aspiration Pneumonia with Acute Respiratory Distress
clinical: Recent aspiration event with acute hypoxia and respiratory failure.
imaging: Bilateral infiltrates in dependent areas on radiograph; CT may show focal consolidation.
report: Aspiration pneumonia with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and acute hypoxic respiratory failure.
treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics; ICU support; intubation if severe; avoid antiemetics that worsen aspiration.

name: Acute Epiglottitis
clinical: Sore throat, dysphagia, drooling with acute upper airway emergency; risk of complete airway obstruction.
imaging: Enlarged epiglottis on lateral neck radiograph or CT; thumbprint sign on radiograph.
report: Markedly thickened epiglottis with edema concerning for acute epiglottitis.
treatment: Avoid aggravating airway; airway team at bedside; intubation if stridor or respiratory distress.

name: Acute Subdural Empyema
clinical: Fever, headache, altered mental status following head injury or infection; rapid neurologic deterioration.
imaging: Crescent-shaped collection between dura and brain with restricted diffusion on MRI.
report: Subdural collection with restricted diffusion and enhancement indicating empyema.
treatment: Neurosurgery consultation; emergent drainage; IV antibiotics after cultures.

name: Necrotizing Fasciitis
clinical: Rapidly spreading skin infection with disproportionate pain and systemic toxicity; high mortality.
imaging: Gas tracking in soft tissue planes with minimal skin findings on CT.
report: Extensive subcutaneous gas in thigh consistent with necrotizing fasciitis.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation for bedside debridement; broad-spectrum antibiotics; likely multiple operations.

name: Traumatic Diaphragmatic Rupture
clinical: Blunt trauma with respiratory compromise; may present acutely or with delayed presentation.
imaging: Bowel loops in thorax on radiograph; CT shows diaphragmatic defect.
report: Left diaphragmatic rupture with herniation of abdominal viscera into chest.
treatment: Thoracic/surgical consultation; operative repair of diaphragmatic defect.

name: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma
clinical: Acute eye pain, blurred vision, halo around lights; can cause permanent blindness if untreated.
imaging: CT shows shallow anterior chamber and lens displacement.
report: Bilateral shallow anterior chambers with lens-corneal contact suggesting angle-closure risk.
treatment: Emergent ophthalmology consultation; topical and systemic glaucoma medications; laser peripheral iridotomy.

name: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion
clinical: Sudden monocular vision loss; treatment window is narrow for visual recovery.
imaging: CTA head shows arterial occlusion; may need immediate catheter intervention.
report: Central retinal artery occlusion with visualization of thrombus on CTA.
treatment: Ophthalmology consultation; ocular massage and paracentesis temporizing; consider intra-arterial thrombolysis.

name: Retrobulbar Hematoma
clinical: Blunt orbital trauma with vision loss and proptosis; risk of compartment syndrome and blindness.
imaging: Blood collection in retrobulbar space with proptosis and increased intraocular pressure.
report: Large retrobulbar hematoma with significant proptosis and optic nerve compression.
treatment: Ophthalmology consultation; lateral canthotomy/cantholysis if compartment syndrome; urgent drainage if needed.

name: Orbital Cellulitis
clinical: Painful proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, fever; risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis and death.
imaging: Orbital inflammation and enlargement on CT; may show abscess formation.
report: Diffuse orbital inflammation with enlargement and fat stranding consistent with cellulitis.
treatment: Emergent ophthalmology consultation; broad-spectrum antibiotics; CT to rule out abscess; possible drainage.

name: Acute Compartment Syndrome (Extremity)
clinical: Pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch; leads to permanent disability if not emergently decompressed.
imaging: MRI shows muscle edema and signal change in affected compartment.
report: Increased T2 signal in anterior tibial compartment consistent with compartment syndrome.
treatment: Orthopedic/surgical emergency consultation; emergent fasciotomy required.

name: Crush Syndrome with Acute Kidney Injury
clinical: Massive crush injury with rhabdomyolysis and renal failure; hyperkalemia and cardiac arrhythmias risk.
imaging: Muscle edema and heterogeneous enhancement in involved compartments on CT.
report: Extensive muscle injury and rhabdomyolysis with hyperenhancing inflamed muscles.
treatment: Aggressive IV hydration, urine alkalinization, dialysis if needed; monitor for hyperkalemia.

name: Fat Embolism Syndrome
clinical: Confusion, dyspnea, petechiae following long bone fracture; can rapidly progress to respiratory failure.
imaging: Bilateral pulmonary infiltrates on chest radiograph; CT shows ground-glass opacities.
report: Bilateral pulmonary infiltrates consistent with fat embolism syndrome.
treatment: Supportive care with ICU admission; oxygen; consider steroids; monitor for multiorgan involvement.

name: Tension Fracture (Long Bone)
clinical: Severe long bone fracture with potential for compartment syndrome and vascular injury.
imaging: Displaced fracture with significant soft tissue swelling and deformity.
report: Displaced comminuted femur fracture with severe soft tissue injury and vascular compromise risk.
treatment: Orthopedic/vascular consultation; traction; likely operative fixation needed.

name: Vascular Injury with Pseudoaneurysm
clinical: Expanding hematoma or pulsatile mass following trauma or procedure; risk of rupture.
imaging: Pseudoaneurysm with arterial flow on Doppler or CTA.
report: Femoral artery pseudoaneurysm with active arterial bleeding and expanding hematoma.
treatment: Vascular surgery/interventional radiology consultation; embolization or operative repair.

name: Acute Arterial Thrombosis
clinical: Acute limb pain and pallor; if untreated leads to tissue necrosis and amputation.
imaging: CTA shows arterial cutoff with distal ischemia; high-grade stenosis or occlusion.
report: Acute thrombotic occlusion of popliteal artery with distal lower leg ischemia.
treatment: Emergent vascular surgery/interventional radiology; catheter thrombectomy or bypass.

name: Acute Limb Ischemia from Embolism
clinical: Sudden-onset limb pain and sensory loss in patient with atrial fibrillation; window for salvage is hours.
imaging: CTA shows arterial cutoff with abrupt cessation of flow.
report: Acute embolic occlusion of superficial femoral artery with acute lower leg ischemia.
treatment: Emergent vascular surgery/interventional radiology; mechanical thrombectomy or surgical embolectomy.

name: Acute Arterial Dissection (Peripheral)
clinical: Sudden limb pain and ischemia; may follow trauma or have spontaneous etiology.
imaging: Intimal flap in peripheral artery with true and false lumens on CTA.
report: Iliac artery dissection with severe stenosis of true lumen and distal ischemia.
treatment: Vascular surgery/interventional radiology consultation; endovascular stent or open repair.

name: Ruptured Artery (Femoral/Brachial)
clinical: Pulsatile expanding hematoma and shock; requires emergent surgical or endovascular intervention.
imaging: Expanding hematoma with arterial bleeding on contrast CT or active flow on ultrasound.
report: Ruptured femoral artery with massive expanding hemorrhage.
treatment: Vascular surgery emergency; operative repair or endovascular intervention; massive transfusion protocol.

name: Acute Pulmonary Hypertension Crisis
clinical: Acute dyspnea and hypoxia in patient with known pulmonary hypertension; risk of cardiogenic shock.
imaging: Massively dilated right ventricle and pulmonary arteries on CT.
report: Acute right heart strain with marked RV/LV ratio indicating acute decompensation.
treatment: ICU admission; inhaled pulmonary vasodilators; IV inotropic support; cardiology consultation.

name: Peritoneal Dialysis Catheter Perforation
clinical: Abdominal pain and peritonitis during dialysis; can result in bowel perforation and sepsis.
imaging: Free air and perforation of viscus in pelvis on CT.
report: Peritoneal dialysis catheter perforation of small bowel with pneumoperitoneum.
treatment: Urgent surgical consultation; operative exploration and repair of perforation.

name: Massive Transfusion-Related Complications
clinical: Acute coagulopathy and shock during massive transfusion; multi-organ failure risk.
imaging: Multiple injuries requiring massive transfusion; complications develop rapidly.
report: Severe polytrauma with ongoing hemorrhage requiring massive transfusion protocol.
treatment: Damage control surgery; balanced resuscitation; FFP and platelets; monitor for TRALI and TACO.

name: Hyperammonemia with Hepatic Encephalopathy
clinical: Altered mental status and confusion in cirrhotic patient; can rapidly progress to coma.
imaging: MRI shows T1 hyperintensity in basal ganglia consistent with chronic hepatic encephalopathy.
report: T1 signal abnormality in globus pallidus and substantia nigra indicating hyperammonemia.
treatment: Lactulose and rifaxomicin; identify precipitant; ICU support if severe; may need intubation.

name: Fulminant Hepatic Failure
clinical: Coagulopathy and encephalopathy without prior liver disease; mortality high without transplant.
imaging: CT shows small liver and ascites; hepatology ultrasound shows reversal of portal flow.
report: Shrunken liver with portal vein flow reversal and ascites consistent with fulminant hepatic failure.
treatment: ICU admission; transplant hepatology consultation; listed for emergent transplant; supportive care.

name: Pylephlebitis (Portal Vein Thrombosis with Sepsis)
clinical: Fever and abdominal pain in patient with intra-abdominal infection; high mortality if missed.
imaging: Portal vein thrombosis with gas in portal system on CT.
report: Portal vein thrombosis with pneumoporvia and septic appearance consistent with pylephlebitis.
treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics; identify source of infection; consider anticoagulation; surgical drainage if needed.

name: Budd-Chiari Syndrome (Acute)
clinical: Right upper quadrant pain and ascites; can present acutely with fulminant hepatic failure.
imaging: Hepatic vein occlusion with echogenic thrombus on ultrasound; CT shows enlarged congested liver.
report: Acute hepatic vein thrombosis with severe hepatic congestion and massive ascites.
treatment: Anticoagulation; interventional radiology for thrombolysis or stent; transplant evaluation if severe.

name: Retroperitoneal Hemorrhage
clinical: Flank pain and hypotension after anticoagulation or trauma; can be rapidly fatal.
imaging: Large heterogeneous fluid collection in retroperitoneal space.
report: Large retroperitoneal hematoma with mass effect on adjacent structures.
treatment: Reverse anticoagulation if appropriate; vascular/surgical consultation; massive transfusion if unstable.

name: Splenic Rupture/Infarction
clinical: Left upper quadrant pain after trauma; risk of hemorrhagic shock.
imaging: Splenic laceration with active bleeding and splenic infarction on CT.
report: Splenic laceration with active arterial bleeding and extensive infarction.
treatment: Surgical or interventional radiology consultation; splenic artery embolization vs operative splenectomy.

name: Liver Injury with Massive Hemorrhage
clinical: Blunt abdominal trauma with rapid hemodynamic deterioration.
imaging: Large hepatic laceration with active arterial bleeding and massive hemoperitoneum.
report: Complex right hepatic lobe laceration with active bleeding and free intraperitoneal blood.
treatment: Massive transfusion protocol; trauma surgery consultation; consider angiographic embolization vs operative repair.

name: Acute Compartment Syndrome (Abdomen)
clinical: Severe abdominal distension and pain after massive transfusion or bowel edema; organ failure risk.
imaging: Marked abdominal distension with bowel wall thickening and ischemic changes.
report: Abdominal compartment syndrome with severe bowel wall edema and ischemic changes.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; likely requires decompressive laparotomy.

name: Tension Hemopericardium
clinical: Hypotension and shock after thoracic trauma or percutaneous procedure.
imaging: Large pericardial effusion with right atrial collapse on ultrasound.
report: Massive hemopericardium with diastolic collapse of right atrium indicating tamponade.
treatment: Emergent pericardiocentesis; cardiothoracic surgery consultation; operating room for definitive repair.

name: Tension Hemothorax
clinical: Hypotension and respiratory distress after thoracic trauma; life-threatening emergency.
imaging: Complete opacification of hemithorax with mediastinal shift and lung collapse.
report: Massive tension hemothorax with severe mediastinal shift and complete lung collapse.
treatment: Emergent chest tube placement; massive transfusion; cardiothoracic surgery for source control.

name: Acute Coronary Syndrome with Cardiogenic Shock
clinical: Chest pain with hypotension and pulmonary edema; high mortality without urgent intervention.
imaging: Cardiology ultrasound shows global hypokinesis; CTA shows left main or three-vessel disease.
report: Large anterior myocardial infarction with reduced ejection fraction and cardiogenic shock.
treatment: Cardiology/interventional cardiology consultation; emergent cardiac catheterization for intervention.

name: Acute Ventricular Septal Rupture
clinical: New systolic murmur and shock after myocardial infarction; mortality high if not surgically repaired.
imaging: Cardiac ultrasound shows left-to-right shunt at ventricular level.
report: Acute VSD with left-to-right shunting evident on echocardiography in post-MI period.
treatment: Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consultation; inotropic support; operating room for VSD repair.

name: Septic Shock from Meningococcemia
clinical: Fever, petechial rash, hypotension, altered mental status; rapidly progressive with high mortality.
imaging: CT shows meningeal enhancement; may show adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen).
report: Meningitis with adrenal hemorrhage consistent with Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.
treatment: Emergent antibiotics; ICU admission; vasopressor support; early recognition critical for survival.

name: Acute Epiglottitis in Adult
clinical: Severe throat pain, dysphagia, drooling with rapidly progressive upper airway obstruction.
imaging: Marked epiglottic enlargement and aryepiglottic fold thickening on CT.
report: Severely enlarged epiglottis with aryepiglottic fold thickening concerning for acute epiglottitis.
treatment: Airway team at bedside; secure airway before imaging if suspected; antibiotics; avoid agitation.

name: Retropharyngeal Abscess
clinical: Throat pain, dysphagia, fever with progressive airway compromise; risk of mediastinitis.
imaging: Fluid collection in retropharyngeal space with rim enhancement on CT.
report: Large retropharyngeal abscess with airway displacement and mediastinal extension.
treatment: ENT and surgical consultation; IV antibiotics; percutaneous or surgical drainage.

name: Esophageal Food Impaction
clinical: Dysphagia and chest discomfort; if not relieved can progress to perforation.
imaging: Radiopaque foreign body or bolus in esophagus; may show air-fluid level.
report: Esophageal food impaction at gastroesophageal junction with proximal esophageal dilatation.
treatment: GI consultation; attempt endoscopic removal; monitor for perforation; NPO status.

name: Acute Gastric Ulcer Perforation
clinical: Acute severe epigastric pain with free air; requires emergent surgery.
imaging: Pneumoperitoneum on upright radiograph; CT shows ulcer crater and free air.
report: Perforated gastric ulcer with pneumoperitoneum and peritoneal contamination.
treatment: Emergent surgical consultation; NPO; IV fluids; antibiotics; operative repair.

name: Acute Infectious Endocarditis with Septic Emboli
clinical: Fever and new murmur in IVDU or immunocompromised patient; high mortality without treatment.
imaging: CT shows septic pulmonary emboli and vegetation on cardiac ultrasound.
report: Multiple septic pulmonary emboli consistent with hematogenous spread from endocarditis.
treatment: Blood cultures before antibiotics; cardiology/infectious disease consultation; echo for vegetation.

name: Acute Empyema
clinical: Fever and dyspnea in patient with pneumonia; fails to respond to antibiotics.
imaging: Loculated pleural effusion with pus on ultrasound or CT showing fluid locules.
report: Complex loculated pleural effusion consistent with empyema.
treatment: Pulmonology/thoracic surgery consultation; tube thoracostomy or VATS drainage.

name: Tension Pneumothorax (Occult)
clinical: Respiratory distress in ICU patient on mechanical ventilation; clinical diagnosis before imaging.
imaging: CT shows large pneumothorax with mediastinal shift and vascular compression.
report: Large tension pneumothorax with severe mediastinal shift and vascular compression.
treatment: Emergent needle decompression; chest tube placement; do not delay for imaging.

name: Acute Pericarditis with Tamponade
clinical: Pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea with hypotension; can rapidly progress to cardiovascular collapse.
imaging: Large pericardial effusion with diastolic RA collapse on ultrasound.
report: Massive pericardial effusion with right atrial diastolic collapse indicating tamponade physiology.
treatment: Emergent pericardiocentesis; cardiology consultation; identify underlying etiology.

name: Myocardial Rupture Post-MI
clinical: Sudden hemodynamic collapse and cardiogenic shock in acute MI period.
imaging: Cardiac ultrasound shows pseudoaneurysm or free wall rupture with pericardial effusion.
report: Left ventricular free wall rupture with pseudoaneurysm and hemopericardium.
treatment: Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consultation; inotropic support; operating room for repair.

name: Acute Myocarditis with Cardiogenic Shock
clinical: Chest pain and dyspnea with hypotension and pulmonary edema; often viral prodrome.
imaging: Global wall motion abnormality and reduced ejection fraction on cardiac imaging.
report: Global myocardial edema on MRI consistent with acute myocarditis.
treatment: Cardiology consultation; supportive care; consider IABP or ECMO if cardiogenic shock.

name: Diffuse Axonal Injury
clinical: Severe traumatic brain injury with prolonged coma out of proportion to initial CT findings.
imaging: Small hemorrhagic foci at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, or brainstem; MRI is more sensitive than CT.
report: Findings are concerning for diffuse axonal injury involving deep white matter and corpus callosum.
treatment: ICU-level neurocritical care and ICP management; neurosurgery consultation as indicated by associated injuries.

name: Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis
clinical: Headache, seizure, focal deficit, or altered mental status, especially in hypercoagulable or postpartum patients.
imaging: Hyperdense dural sinus on noncontrast CT or absent venous flow/filling defect on CTV or MRV with possible venous infarct.
report: Dural venous sinus thrombosis involving the transverse/sigmoid sinus with associated venous edema or hemorrhagic infarction.
treatment: Urgent anticoagulation unless contraindicated; neurology consultation and escalation for endovascular therapy if deteriorating.

name: Placental Abruption
clinical: Pregnant patient with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, or fetal distress after trauma or spontaneously.
imaging: Retroplacental hematoma or placental thickening on ultrasound; MRI can better define concealed hemorrhage.
report: Placental abruption with retroplacental hematoma and imaging concern for ongoing maternal-fetal compromise.
treatment: Immediate obstetric evaluation, maternal stabilization, and urgent delivery planning based on fetal and maternal status.
